    • Problem 1: VAE Trade-offs

Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

The Problem of Intractable Posteriors

In probabilistic machine learning, we often posit that our observable data \(x\) is generated by some hidden, unobservable latent variables \(z\).

To perform inference, we need to calculate the posterior probability of the latents given the data, using Bayes’ Theorem:

\[ p(z | x) = \frac{p(x | z) p(z)}{p(x)} \]

The denominator \(p(x)\) is called the Evidence. To compute it, we must marginalize (integrate) over every possible configuration of the latent variables \(z\):

\[ p(x) = \int p(x | z) p(z) \, dz \]

For modern deep learning models, \(z\) is continuous and high-dimensional. This integral is mathematically intractable to compute. If we can’t compute the evidence \(p(x)\), we can’t compute the posterior \(p(z | x)\), and we cannot train our model.

Enter Variational Inference

Instead of trying to compute the exact intractable posterior \(p(z | x)\), Variational Inference (VI) proposes a workaround: let’s create a separate, simple probability distribution \(q_\phi(z | x)\) (like a Gaussian) parameterized by a neural network \(\phi\), and try to make it as close to the true posterior as possible.

We measure “closeness” using KL Divergence: \[ D_{KL} \big( q_\phi(z | x) \big\| p(z | x) \big) \]

But wait—to minimize this KL divergence, we still need to evaluate the true posterior \(p(z | x)\), which we just established is intractable! This is where the magic of the ELBO comes in.

Deriving the ELBO

Let’s expand the KL divergence equation using the definition of conditional probability \(p(z|x) = p(x,z) / p(x)\):

\[ \begin{aligned} D_{KL} \big( q_\phi(z | x) \big\| p(z | x) \big) &= \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \log \frac{q_\phi(z | x)}{p(z | x)} \right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \log q_\phi(z | x) - \log p(z | x) \right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \log q_\phi(z | x) - \log \left( \frac{p(x, z)}{p(x)} \right) \right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \log q_\phi(z | x) - \log p(x, z) + \log p(x) \right] \end{aligned} \]

Since the evidence \(\log p(x)\) does not depend on \(z\), we can pull it completely out of the expectation:

\[ D_{KL} \big( q_\phi(z | x) \big\| p(z | x) \big) = \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \log q_\phi(z | x) - \log p(x, z) \right] + \log p(x) \]

Now, rearrange the terms to solve for the log evidence \(\log p(x)\):

\[ \log p(x) = \underbrace{ \mathbb{E}_{q} \left[ \log p(x, z) - \log q_\phi(z | x) \right] }_{\text{ELBO}} + \underbrace{ D_{KL} \big( q_\phi(z | x) \big\| p(z | x) \big) }_{\ge 0} \]

Because KL Divergence is strictly non-negative, the remaining term mathematically serves as an absolute lower bound for the evidence. We call this the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO):

\[ \log p(x) \ge \text{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z | x)} \left[ \log \left( \frac{p(x, z)}{q_\phi(z | x)} \right) \right] \]

The Core Insight

We cannot compute \(\log p(x)\) directly. However, if we maximize the ELBO, we are simultaneously doing two things: 1. Pushing up the lower bound of our data log-likelihood \(\log p(x)\) (making our model better at generating the data). 2. Minimizing the KL divergence between our approximate posterior \(q_\phi\) and the true posterior \(p\), since \(\log p(x)\) is fixed for a given dataset!

Machine Learning Context: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)

The ELBO can be rewritten into its most famous form, which is directly used as the loss function for Variational Autoencoders:

\[ \text{ELBO} = \underbrace{ \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z | x)} [\log p_\theta(x | z)] }_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace{ D_{KL} \big( q_\phi(z | x) \big\| p(z) \big) }_{\text{Regularization Term}} \]

  1. Reconstruction Term: The decoder network \(p_\theta(x|z)\) attempts to perfectly reconstruct the original data \(x\) from the latent vector \(z\). (This is equivalent to Mean Squared Error or Binary Cross-Entropy).
  2. Regularization Term: The encoder network \(q_\phi(z|x)\) is penalized if its predicted latent distribution strays too far from the standard prior distribution \(p(z)\) (typically a standard normal \(\mathcal{N}(0, I)\)). This forces the latent space to be continuous, smooth, and easily sampleable.

Practice Problems

Problem 1: VAE Trade-offs

In the Variational Autoencoder ELBO equation, explain the competing roles of the Reconstruction Term and the KL Regularization term. What happens to the learned latent space if the regularization weight is set to exactly zero?
